O. |last3=Kvaløy |first3=K. |last4=Røv |first4=N. |last5=Haring |first5=E. |date=2005-01-01 |title=Molecular phylogeny of the hawk-eagles (genus Spizaetus) |url=https://repository.naturalis.nl/pub/210823 |journal=Zoologische Mededelingen |language=en |volume=79 |issue=3 |pages=179–180 |issn=0024-0672}}</ref><ref name="Amadon">{{Cite journal |last=Amadon |first=Dean |date=July 1953 |title=Remarks on the Asiatic hawk-eagles of the genus Spizaetu |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1474-919X.1953.tb00709.x |journal=Ibis |language=en |volume=95 |issue=3 |pages=492–500 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1953.tb00709.x |issn=0019-1019|url-access=subscription }}</ref> As is typical of hawk-eagles, the mountain hawk-eagle is a forest dwelling opportunistic predator who readily varies its prey selection between [[bird]]s, [[mammal]]s and [[reptile]]s along with other [[vertebrate]]s.<ref name="Naoroji">{{Cite book |last=Naoroji |first=Rishad |title=Birds of prey of the indian subcontinent |date=2006 |publisher=Christopher Helm |isbn=978-0-7136-6324-2 |location=London}}</ref> Although classified currently as a [[least-concern species]] due its persistence over a rather wide distribution, this species is often quite rare and scarce and seems to be decreasing, especially in response to large-scale habitat degradation and [[deforestation]].<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /><ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name="Asai">{{Cite journal |last1=Asai |first1=Shigeki |last2=Yamamoto |first2=Yoshihiro |last3=Yamagishi |first3=Satoshi |date=June 2006 |title=Genetic diversity and extent of gene flow in the endangered Japanese population of Hodgson's hawk-eagle, Spizaetus nipalensis |journal=Bird Conservation International |language=en |volume=16 |issue=2 |pages=113–129 |doi=10.1017/S0959270906000050 |issn=0959-2709 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Description==
===Size and taxonomy===
[[File:Hawkeagle.jpg|thumb|Mountain hawk-eagles are the largest and most robust of the hawk-eagles in the genus ''Nisaetus''.]]
The mountain hawk-eagle is a large raptor and fairly large eagle. Although described not infrequently as "slim", it is usually perceptibly bulkier and more massive than most other members of its genus. It is seemingly the largest member of the 10 currently recognized species in the genus '' [[Nisaetus]]'', notwithstanding the recently recognized [[Flores hawk-eagle]] (''Nisaetus floris'') (which was separated from the [[changeable hawk-eagle]]). The latter [[critically endangered]] island hawk-eagle seems to be of broadly similar size (weight is unknown), albeit with shorter wings, however the Flores species seems to be linearly outmatched by and slimmer in build the largest mountain hawk-eagle.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref>Collaerts, P., Collaerts, E., Verbelen, P., & Trainor, C. R. (2013). ''Discovery of the critically endangered Flores Hawk Eagle Nisaetus floris on Alor island, Indonesia''. Birding ASIA, 19, 48-51.</ref> The mountain hawk-eagle attains a total length of {{convert|69|to|84|cm|in|abbr=on}} and a [[wingspan]] of {{convert|134|to|175|cm|ftin|0|abbr=on}}. Like most birds of prey, females are larger on average than the male, with a typical size difference of 3-8%, though it can rarely range up to a 21% difference. Although its wings are relatively short compared to eagles of open country, it has the longest wings of any of the hawk-eagles, even relative to their size.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name="Gjershaug">{{Cite journal |last1=Gjershaug |first1=J. O. |last2=Diserud |first2=O. H. |last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. C. |last4=Warakagoda |first4=D. |date=2008-06-12 |title=An overlooked threatened species of eagle: Legge's Hawk Eagle Nisaetus kelaarti (Aves: Accipitriformes) |url=https://biotaxa.org/Zootaxa/article/view/zootaxa.1792.1.4 |journal=Zootaxa |volume=1792 |issue=1 |page=54 |doi=10.11646/zootaxa.1792.1.4 |issn=1175-5334 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Mountain hawk-eagles have a short but strong bill, long and often erect crest (though can also be very short), short wings, a longish three-banded tail, feathered legs and powerful feet. It is usually rather unobtrusive, perching rather upright inside of canopy, with its wing-tips coming to less than one-fifth down the tail.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> There are two currently recognized races of the mountain hawk-eagle: the nominate subspecies (''N. n. nipalensis'') and the subspecies native to [[Japan]] (''N. n. orientalis''). The nominate race is found throughout mainland range and includes the likely dubious southeast Chinese races of ''N. n. fokiensis'' and ''N. n. whiteheadi''. The average total length of the nominate subspecies is estimated to be {{convert|72|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Ali/> Among standard measurements in the nominate race, the [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] of males ranges from {{convert|410|to|465|mm|in|abbr=on}} while the female's ranges from {{convert|445|to|508|mm|in|abbr=on}}. In both sexes, the tail ranges from {{convert|279|to|314.3|mm|in|abbr=on}} and the [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]] from {{convert|100|to|119.9|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Gjershaug/> 9 males of the nominate race were found to average {{convert|437.2|mm|in|abbr=on}} in wing length, {{convert|281.3|mm|in|abbr=on}} in tail length, {{convert|38.7|mm|in|abbr=on}} in [[Claw|hallux claw]] length (the large rear talon often utilized by accipitrids as a killing tool), {{convert|110.3|mm|in|abbr=on}} in tarsus length and {{convert|30.3|mm|in|abbr=on}} in [[Beak|bill]] length. 13 females of the nominate were found to average {{convert|479.2|mm|in|abbr=on}} in wing length, {{convert|297.9|mm|in|abbr=on}} in tail length, {{convert|46|mm|in|abbr=on}} in hallux claw length, {{convert|114.8|mm|in|abbr=on}} in tarsus length and {{convert|33.9|mm|in|abbr=on}} in bill length.<ref name= Gjershaug/> One unsexed mountain hawk-eagle from northern India was found to weigh {{convert|1.83|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Ali/> A single male from the [[Yangtze]] area of east-central [[China]] was found to have weighed {{convert|2.8|kg|lb|abbr=on}} while two females from there weighed {{convert|2.95|and|3.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, suggesting size increases further north in this subspecies in accordance with [[Bergmann's rule]].<ref name="CRC">{{Cite book |title=CRC handbook of avian body masses |date=2008 |publisher=CRC Press |isbn=978-1-4200-6444-5 |editor-last=Dunning |editor-first=John Barnard |edition=2 |location=Boca Raton}}</ref><ref>Piechocki, R. (1968). ''Zur Avifauna der Mongolei, I. Non-Passerines''. Mitteilungen aus dem Zoologischen Museum in Berlin, 44, 149-292.</ref>

The Japanese race averages about 9% larger than mainland race, and also has a proportionately longer tail and longer wings.<ref>{{cite journal|title= Gizzard Nematodiasis in Japanese Mountain Hawk Eagle (Spizaetus nipalensis)|jstor=1592923|last1=Nakamura|first1=Kikuyasu|last2=Ohyama|first2=Takayuki|last3=Saito|first3=Asuka|last4=Yamada|first4=Manabu|last5=Imada|first5=Tadao|last6=Mase|first6=Masaji|journal=Avian Diseases|year=2001|volume=45|issue=3|pages=751–754|doi=10.2307/1592923|pmid=11569755}}</ref> The populations from [[Taiwan]] and the possible ones in [[Hainan]] are also probably part of this race.<ref name= Naoroji/> ''N. n. orientalis'' is generally paler than the nominate race with less heavy markings below. The underside in this race has a paler ground colour against much browner and darker barring, often showing less of the warmer or rufous tones typical of mainland mountain hawk-eagles. The throat often has reduced blackish streaking compared to mainland birds and the upper chest can be whitish and nearly unmarked. ''N. n. orientalis'' has black mottled or light streaked wing-linings and a small, often vestigial crest compared to the rather ample one of the mainland birds. Sometimes, ''N. n. orientalis'' is hypothesized to be a separate species.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref>{{Cite book |last=Shimba |first=Tadao |title=A photographic guide to the birds of Japan and North-East Asia |date=2007 |publisher=Yale University Press |isbn=978-0-300-13556-5 |location=New Haven}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Nishida |first1=C. |last2=Ishijima |first2=J. |last3=Ishishita |first3=S. |last4=Yamada |first4=K. |last5=Griffin |first5=D.K. |last6=Yamazaki |first6=T. |last7=Matsuda |first7=Y. |date=2013 |title=Karyotype Reorganization with Conserved Genomic Compartmentalization in Dot-Shaped Microchromosomes in the Japanese Mountain Hawk-Eagle (Nisaetus nipalensis orientalis, Accipitridae) |journal=Cytogenetic and Genome Research |language=en |volume=141 |issue=4 |pages=284–294 |doi=10.1159/000352067 |issn=1424-8581 |pmid= 23838459 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Among standard measurements [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] of males ranges from {{convert|470|to|518|mm|in|abbr=on}} while the female's ranges from {{convert|500|to|540|mm|in|abbr=on}}. In both sexes, the tail ranges from {{convert|325|to|395|mm|in|abbr=on}} and the [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]] from {{convert|104|to|128.9|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name="Brown">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Leslie |title=Eagles, hawks and falcons of the world |last2=Amadon |first2=Dean |date=1989 |publisher=Wellfleet Press |isbn=978-1-55521-472-2 |location=Secaucus, NJ}}</ref><ref name= Yamazaki>Yamazaki, T. (2010). ''Mountain Hawk-Eagle Kuma-Taka (Jpn) Nisaetus nipalensis''. Bird Research News, 7 (12): 6-7.</ref> From a sample of unknown size from the [[Suzuka Mountains]], males of ''N. n. orientalis'' were found to average {{convert|71.4|cm|in|abbr=on}} and females {{convert|76.3|cm|in|abbr=on}} in total length. From the same sample, males had a mean wing chord length of {{convert|488|mm|in|abbr=on}}, tail length of {{convert|342|mm|in|abbr=on}}, [[Beak|culmen]] length of {{convert|34|mm|in|abbr=on}} and tarsus length of {{convert|113|mm|in|abbr=on}}. Meanwhile, females had a mean wing chord length of {{convert|516|mm|in|abbr=on}}, tail length of {{convert|356|mm|in|abbr=on}}, culmen length of {{convert|37|mm|in|abbr=on}} and tarsus length of {{convert|118.2|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Yamazaki/> Perhaps most surprisingly, the Suzuka mountain birds were not noticeably discrepant in body mass from known weights of mainland mountain hawk-eagles, especially similar to that of the apparently larger hawk-eagles from east-central China. The Suzuka sample as above found males to weigh from {{convert|2.2|to|2.7|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, with an average of {{convert|2.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, while females were found to weigh from {{convert|2.5|to|3.9|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, with an average of {{convert|3.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= CRC/><ref name= Yamazaki/> Another Japanese survey found the smallest male to weigh {{convert|1.68|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, the lightest known weight known anywhere for the species.<ref>Enomoto, Y. (1941). ''Yachobinran (Handbook of wild birds)''. Wild Bird Society of Japan, Osaka Branch, Osaka.</ref>
[[File:Naturalis Biodiversity Center - RMNH.ART.461 - Spizaetus nipalensis - Yūshi Ishizaki - Cock Blomhoff Collection - pencil drawing - water colour.jpg|thumb|Drawing of the Japanese race by Yūshi Ishizaki, from the 1830s.]]
At one time largish hawk-eagles found in [[Sri Lanka]] and [[Western Ghats|southwestern India]] was deemed to be part of the mountain hawk-eagle species under the subspecies ''N. n. kelaarti''. A 2008 study based on the geographic isolation and differences in call suggest that this be treated as a full species, ''Nisaetus kelaarti'', [[Legge's hawk-eagle]]. The full species status of Legge's hawk-eagle appears to be further supported by DNA studies, with an average difference in [[mitochondrial DNA]] of 4.3% (usually the minimum difference to differentiate species is considered to be 1.5%).<ref name= Gjershaug/><ref name="Gjershaug, J. O. 2006">{{Cite web |last=Gjershaug |first=Jan Ove |date=2006 |title=Taxonomy and conservation status of hawk-eagles (Genus Nisaetus) in south-east Asia |url=https://ntnuopen.ntnu.no/ntnu-xmlui/handle/11250/244615 |access-date=2024-01-04}}</ref><ref name="Lerner">{{Cite journal |last1=Lerner |first1=Heather |last2=Christidis |first2=Les |last3=Gamauf |first3=Anita |last4=Griffiths |first4=Carole |last5=Haring |first5=Elisabeth |last6=Huddleston |first6=Christopher J. |last7=Kabra |first7=Sonia |last8=Kocum |first8=Annett |last9=Krosby |first9=Meade |last10=KvaløY |first10=Kirsti |last11=Mindell |first11=David |last12=Rasmussen |first12=Pamela |last13=RøV |first13=Nils |last14=Wadleigh |first14=Rachel |last15=Wink |first15=Michael |date=2017-01-09 |title=Phylogeny and new taxonomy of the Booted Eagles (Accipitriformes: Aquilinae) |url=https://www.mapress.com/zt/article/view/zootaxa.4216.4.1 |journal=Zootaxa |volume=4216 |issue=4 |doi=10.11646/zootaxa.4216.4.1 |issn=1175-5334 |pmid= 28183111|hdl=11250/2443953 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Although extremely isolated in distribution from true mountain hawk-eagles, Legge's hawk-eagle is physically distinct as well, often being much paler and less marked below with the throat stripes characteristics of the mountain species often absent (occasionally faint stripes may manifest) being instead largely plain buff about the throat. Like mainland mountain hawk-eagles, Legge's hawk-eagles have a strong crest. The hand in flight on a Legge's is often plain buff in colour (or with some very faint streaking) and the banded wing feathers are rather faded. Legge's hawk-eagle appears to be about 10% smaller than mountain hawk-eagles and was found to differ in almost all bodily proportions from mountain hawk-eagle, with relatively smaller wings but the smaller species also has a larger bill and larger talons than the mountain hawk-eagle.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Gjershaug/>
[[File:Nisaetus nipalensis 3.jpg|thumb|left|A subadult mountain hawk-eagle in flight in [[Singapore]].]]

===Colouring and confusion species===
[[File:Nisaetus nipalensis 174790944.jpg|thumb|left|A mountain hawk-eagle adult.]]
Adult mountain hawk-eagles are dark brown above with slightly paler edges, which tend to be clearest on median and greater coverts. On adults, the head is fairly rusty above with strong black streaks, though the volume of streaks tends to decrease on the neck, which in turn may suggest a rufous collar. The crest is largely black with a small buffy tip. Their tail is grey-brown, with a whitish tip and rather obscure blackish banding above. The malar area and throat are marked with blackish, ragged and sparse but rather bold stripes which contrast with the rest of their underside which is predominantly barred with rufous over a [[countershading|whitish]] ground colour. The barring continues, though the white base colour narrows and the rufous becomes a somewhat browner hue, down to the crissum and the legs. In some cases, the colour about the legs has appeared variously chestnut or even blackish.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> The underside of the tail is boldly banded with blackish and grey. The juvenile mountain hawk-eagle is also dark brown above but usually has clear cream to whitish feather edges causing the wing coverts to have a scaled effect; meanwhile, the feather bases of median and greater coverts form tawnier mid-wing patches. The juvenile's tail is thinly banded alternately with lighter and darker brown but usually have a whitish tip like the tail of the adult. The juvenile mountain hawk-eagle's underside is all plain buffy to tawny. The underside colour also extends to the head and part of the neck flanks while the crown, cheek, nape and hind-neck all streaked with dark brown. The crest is black with a small white tip. The juvenile leg feathers are whitish. The markings on the underside begin to develop by the 2nd year, starting from the flanks and gradually increasing inward to the breast, but the young hawk-eagles are still quite paler below until their 3rd year, which is also when the tail starts to resemble the adults. Full adult plumage is attained at no later than the 4th year.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name="Ali">{{Cite book |last1=Ali |first1=Sálim |title=Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan: Together with Those of Bangladesh, Nepal, Sikkim, Bhutan and Sri Lanka10 Volume Set |last2=Ripley |first2=Sidney Dillon |date=2002-01-28 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-565506-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>Clark, W. S. (2004). ''Wave moult of the primaries in accipitrid raptors, and its use in ageing immatures''. Raptors worldwide, 795-804.</ref> Adults have golden or even yellowish-orange eyes, with juveniles having pale bluish-grey to pale yellow eyes. In the adult the [[Beak|cere]] is blackish-grey, while in juveniles it is dull-grey. In all ages, the feet range from dull yellow to yellowish white.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> In flight, it is notable for its rather prominent head and relatively short rounded wings, an effect emphasized by their broad hands and bulging secondaries, which tend to pinch in at the rear bases. The mountain hawk-eagle is capable of fast, agile flight "with astonishing maneuverability". They usually glide with powerful, shallow beats interspersed with glides on level wings, but soaring birds hold their wings in a shallow [[Dihedral angle|V]], pressed slightly forward. The wing linings of adults are a rusty similar to flank coloring, becoming paler on forepart and marked with dusky mottling which becomes darker mid-wing. Blackish-brown bars are apparent over greyish ground colour on the secondaries, the feathers here relatively broadly barred with blackish while the primaries are whiter based and darker tipped. Flying juveniles are fairly heavily mottled with white above. Juveniles show more buff to tawny colour below extending to their wing linings while the wing-tips are black, in some cases extending to primary coverts form a vague carpal arc. Juvenile flight feathers are whitish grey with thin and rather faded looking dusky barring, with less white showing at the base of the primaries. Juveniles in flight usually evidence a less distinct subterminal band than do adults.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name="Brazil">{{Cite book |last=Brazil |first=Mark |title=Birds of East Asia: China, Taiwan, Korea, Japan, and Russia |date=2009 |publisher=Princeton university press |isbn=978-0-691-13926-5 |series=Princeton field guides |location=Princeton}}</ref>
[[File:Mountain hawk-eagle.jpg|thumb|Close-up of a mountain hawk-eagle from China.]]
Confusion of mountain hawk-eagle in all plumages is possible with pale morph [[changeable hawk-eagle]]s (''Nisaetus cirrhatus''). However, the latter species only has a vestigal crest in most areas of overlap from northern India to [[southeast Asia]]. Furthermore, the changeable is a slighter, more slender bird with a relatively longer tail. The latter species also has narrower wings with more even trailing edges. While soaring, changeable hawk-eagles tend to have flatter wing shape than mountain hawk-eagles. Changeable adults also have streaking rather than heavy rusty barring on their underside, apart from subtle parts of wing linings and flanks, and also have narrower tail bars. In flight, the changeable also has clear white base to their primaries and less whitish on the rump when seen from above. Juveniles of the two species are more easily mistaken but wing proportions always differ, the mountain juveniles usually appear perceptibly bulkier and changeable juveniles (of relevant races) are generally much paler, rather than warm buffy to tawny, on the head and underparts.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref>{{Cite book |title=A photographic guide to the birds of India: and the Indian subcontinent, including Pakistan, Nepal, Bhutan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka & the Maldives |date=2002 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-0-691-11496-5 |editor-last=Grewal |editor-first=Bikram |location=Princeton, NJ |editor-last2=Harvey |editor-first2=Bill |editor-last3=Pfister |editor-first3=Otto}}</ref> The mountain hawk-eagle also overlaps somewhat in range, in [[southeast Asia]], with [[Blyth's hawk-eagle]] (''Nisaetus alboniger'') and [[Wallace's hawk-eagle]]s (''Nisaetus nanus'') but both are much smaller and different in multiple ways (especially the bold black-and-white of adult Blyth's). Another, albeit unlikely, potential source of confusion for the mountain hawk-eagle is with [[Jerdon's baza]] (''Aviceda jerdoni''), which is far smaller, and of a far more compact and chunky build.  The baza is somewhat similar in marking to adult mountain hawk-eagles, but the baza lacks feathered legs and has relatively much longer and differently shaped wings. Mountain hawk-eagles can usually be told from the slighter, smaller [[crested honey buzzard]] (''Pernis ptilorhynchus''), beyond the latter being polymorphic, as even most similarly plumaged individual honey buzzards have bare legs, much smaller and slimmer head and bill with a longer neck and deeper wing beats on relatively longer, more slender wings.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref>{{Cite book |last1=MacKinnon |first1=John |title=A field guide to the birds of China |last2=Philipps |first2=Karen |last3=Showler |first3=David |date=2006 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-854940-6 |edition=4 |location=Oxford New York}}</ref><ref name= Brazil2>Brazil, M. A., & Hanawa, S. (1991). ''The status and distribution of diurnal raptors in Japan''. Birds of Prey Bulletin, 4, 175-238.</ref>

===Vocalisation===
Mountain hawk-eagles are silent apart from their breeding season. Their call is a shrill treble note, with a quality often compared to a [[Tin whistle|penny whistle]].<ref name="Ali2">{{Cite book |last=Ali |first=Sálim |title=The book of Indian birds |date=1997 |publisher=Oxford Univ. Pr |isbn=978-0-19-563731-1 |edition=12 |location=Mumbai}}</ref> Their typical call is often likened to the ''klu-weet-weet'' of a [[green sandpiper]] (''Tringa ochropus'') or the ''kee-kikik'' of the [[common green magpie]] (''Cissa chinensis'').<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Gjershaug/> Sometimes the call is written in Japan as ''pie-pie-pie'' or ''pipipi''.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Brazil2/> The hawk-eagles, including both members of a [[breeding pair]], may call both in flight and while perched. Another call of a rapid bubbling quality, which is considered comparable to that of the [[little grebe]] (''Tachybaptus ruficollis''), is probably produced only during sky-dances.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Ali2/> A study in [[Taiwan]] diagnosed seven call types consisting of different quality trills in Taiwan during the breeding cycle, including different calls emitted during flight or while perched and food-begging or alarm calls by nestlings.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Huang |first1=Yung-Kun |last2=Chen |first2=Hou-Chun |last3=Chiang |first3=Po-Jen |last4=Sun |first4=Yuan-Hsun |date=August 2021 |title=Vocal behavior of the Mountain Hawk-Eagle Nisaetus nipalensis in Taiwan |journal=Global Ecology and Conservation |language=en |volume=28 |article-number=e01655 |doi=10.1016/j.gecco.2021.e01655 |doi-access=free|bibcode=2021GEcoC..2801655H }}</ref> The calls of mountain hawk-eagles are said to be expertly mimicked by [[drongo]]s in some parts of the range.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Goodale |first1=Eben |last2=Ratnayake |first2=Chaminda P. |last3=Kotagama |first3=Sarath W. |date=March 2014 |editor-last=Janik |editor-first=V. |title=Vocal Mimicry of Alarm-Associated Sounds by a Drongo Elicits Flee and Mobbing Responses from Other Species that Participate in Mixed-Species Bird Flocks |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/eth.12202 |journal=Ethology |language=en |volume=120 |issue=3 |pages=266–274 |doi=10.1111/eth.12202 |bibcode=2014Ethol.120..266G |issn=0179-1613|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ratnayake |first1=Chaminda P. |last2=Goodale |first2=Eben |last3=Kotagama |first3=Sarath W. |date=January 2010 |title=Two sympatric species of passerine birds imitate the same raptor calls in alarm contexts |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/s00114-009-0617-7 |journal=Naturwissenschaften |language=en |volume=97 |issue=1 |pages=103–108 |doi=10.1007/s00114-009-0617-7 |issn=0028-1042 |pmid=19937310|bibcode=2010NW.....97..103R |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Mountain Hawk-eagle Spizaetus nipalensis (Hodgson, 1836) (16169922527).jpg|thumb|left|Mountain hawk-eagles often live in cooler, more temperate habitats including deciduous forest than other hawk-eagles.]]
Both the northern and southern limits of this widely found raptor are surprisingly poorly known to this day, with historic records suggesting that the species may take up residence hundreds of kilometers north of its accepted range and year-around reports of this species from areas formerly considered only to be visited by wintering migrant or vagrant hawk-eagles.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /><ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/> The mountain hawk-eagle is distributed through the [[Himalayas]], extending from northeastern [[Pakistan]] through [[north India]] in at least the states of central [[Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)|Jammu and Kashmir]], [[Himachal Pradesh]] and [[Uttarakhand]], continuing into [[Nepal]], [[Bhutan]] to northern [[Assam]] thence southward into the north and east [[Burma|Burmese]] highlands, west and peninsular [[Thailand]], also the northern parts of [[Laos]] and probably [[Vietnam]]. Their range continues eastward into southeastern [[China]] where they may be found in [[Yunnan]], [[Guangxi]] and [[Guangdong Province]] northward in the east to the lower reaches of the [[Yangtze]] in [[Anhui]] and [[Zhejiang]]. Their range also extends to [[Hainan]], an offshore island of China. Mountain hawk-eagles are additionally found in the island nations of [[Taiwan]] and [[Japan]], with the highest concentration known on northern islands such [[Hokkaido]] but they may be found on nearly all the islands of Japan.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /><ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Yamazaki/><ref name= Brazil2/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dixit |first1=Soham |last2=Joshi |first2=Viral |last3=Barve |first3=Sahas |date=2016-04-17 |title=Bird diversity of the Amrutganga Valley, Kedarnath, Uttarakhand, India with an emphasis on the elevational distribution of species |url=https://checklist.pensoft.net/article/19476/ |journal=Check List |volume=12 |issue=2 |page=1874 |doi=10.15560/12.2.1874 |issn=1809-127X |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>Colijn, B. K., Lambert, F., Noske, R., Trainor, C., Coates, B. J., Bishop, K. D. & del Hoyo, J. (2005). ''Ornithological surveys of two reserves in Guangxi province, China, 2004–2005''.</ref><ref>Lok, C. B. P., Shing, L. K., Jian-Feng, Z., & Wen-Ba, S. (2005). ''Notable bird records from Bawangling national nature reserve, Hainan island, China''. Forktail, 21, 33.</ref><ref>Fei, W., Luming, L., Jianyun, G., Dao, Y., Wanzhao, H., Ting, Y., & Xiaojun, Y. (2015). ''Birds of the Ailao Mountains, Yunnan province, China''. Forktail, (31), 47-54.</ref><ref>Le Manh Hung, Robbins, M. B., Rice, N. H., & Roldan-Pina, D. (2015). ''Survey of the avifauna of Van Ban Nature Reserve, Lao Cai province, Vietnam''. Forktail, (31), 103-106.</ref> The mountain hawk-eagle has been recorded under the status of "rare breeder" in areas much farther north than is conventionally accepted as part of their range, such as far eastern [[Mongolia]] and the landlocked, extreme southern part of the [[Russian Far East]] such as in [[Primorsky Krai]]. To this date, the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] has not updated the range maps for mountain hawk-eagles to reflect the species' presence in these areas, although their status as continual breeders here may still need confirmation.<ref>Gombobaatar, S., Samiya, D., & Baillie, J. M. (2012). ''Bird Red List and Its Future Development in Mongolia''.</ref><ref>Gombobaatar, B. (2012). ''The Mongolian Bird Taxonomy and Rarities Committee and its role''. Ornis Mongolica, 1: 74-75.</ref><ref>Gorchakov, G. A., & Nechaev, V. A. (1994). ''Crested eagle, Spizaetus nipalensis (Hodgson, 1836) (Accipitridae, Aves),-a new nesting species of Russian fauna''. Biulletenj Moskovskogo Obscestva Ispytatelei Prirody-Otdel Biologitcheskij, 99(4), 15-17.</ref><ref>Karyakin, I.V. 2007. ''[New record of the Mountain Hawk Eagle nesting in Primorye, Russia]''. Raptors Conservation, 9: 63-64.</ref> Through much of their range, mountain hawk-eagles are typically sedentary but both adults and young hawk-eagles sometimes also disperse in descent from higher grounds in winter and it may be characterized as a partial migrant. There are several recorded cases of the species wandering in north India down into [[Indo-Gangetic plains]]. In [[Bhutan]], fragmentary information suggest short-distance altitudinal movements are not infrequent.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Juhant |first1=Matías A. |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/identifier/9781316335420%23CN-bp-6/type/book_part |title=Raptor Migration across and around the Himalayas |last2=Bildstein |first2=Keith L. |date=2017-04-06 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-1-316-33542-0 |editor-last=Prins |editor-first=Herbert H. T. |edition=1 |pages=98–116 |doi=10.1017/9781316335420.009 |editor-last2=Namgail |editor-first2=Tsewang}}</ref><ref>Feijen, C., Feijen, H. & Schulten, G. (2005). ''Raptor migration in Bhutan: incidental observations''. BirdingAsia, 1 (3): 61-62.</ref> Relatively low volumes of migrant mountain hawk-eagles were detected in [[Nepal]], however.<ref>Subedi, T. (2014). ''East to West Migration of Steppe Eagle Aquila nipalensis and other Raptors in Nepal: Abundance, Timing and Age Class Determination''. National Birds of Prey Trust, UK.</ref> With a fair amount of consistency, the hawk-eagles found in the northern part of [[southeast Asia]] range into more lowland areas of [[Burma]], eastern [[Thailand]] and peninsular [[Malaysia]].  There are also similar movements to lowlands in [[Japan]] with some Japanese ones moving to the [[Korean peninsula]]. In some of the areas above such as [[Thailand]] and [[Malaysia]] (mainly far northern part of country), year-around reports of mountain hawk-eagles may suggest small, isolated pockets of residency and/or breeding occurring.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Round |first=Philip D. |date=1983 |title=Some Recent Bird Records from Northern Thailand |url=http://archive.org/details/nhbss_031_2f_round_somerecentbirdreco |journal=Nat. Hist. Bull. Siam Soc |language=English |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=123–138}}</ref><ref>Chye, L. K. (2012). ''Current status and distribution of diurnal raptors in Malaysia''. Ornis Mongolica, 1, 52-59.</ref> Mountain hawk-eagles have been recorded as a vagrant in [[Hong Kong]] and [[Cambodia]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Lewthwaite |first=Richard |date=1989 |title=Mountain Hawk Eagle at Luk Keng - a new species for Hong Kong |url=https://digitalrepository.lib.hku.hk/catalog/4j03g085c#?c=&m=&s=&cv=&xywh=-1104%252C-129%252C3707%252C2575 |journal=Hong Kong Bird Report |language=en |pages=75–78}}</ref><ref>Poole, C. (2000). ''Mountain Hawk-eagle confirmed in Cambodia''. Cambodia Bird News, 4: 33.</ref> Broader vagrancy has been reported in the case of a mountain hawk-eagle that turned up in the island of [[Borneo]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Burner |first1=Ryan C. |last2=Chua |first2=Vivien L. |last3=Brady |first3=Matthew L. |last4=van Els |first4=Paul |last5=Steinhoff |first5=Philip O. M. |last6=Rahman |first6=Mustafa Abdul |last7=Sheldon |first7=Frederick H. |date=2016-06-30 |title=An ornithological survey of Gunung Mulu National Park, Sarawak, Malaysian Borneo |url=https://bioone.org/journals/the-wilson-journal-of-ornithology/volume-128/issue-2/wils-128-02-242-254.1/An-ornithological-survey-of-Gunung-Mulu-National-Park-Sarawak-Malaysian/10.1676/wils-128-02-242-254.1.full |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=128 |issue=2 |page=242 |doi=10.1676/wils-128-02-242-254.1 |issn=1559-4491 |jstor=26429727|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The mountain hawk-eagle tends to reside in dense hill and montane forests at any point up to the [[tree line]]. They are mainly found in various wooded [[foothills]]. Typically, [[Primary forest|primary evergreen or mixed forests]] are preferred, with the presence of nearby [[stream]]s a plus. The central part of the range falls in [[Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests|subtropical broadleaf forest]] made cooler by their high elevations but they can range anywhere from [[Temperate broadleaf and mixed forests|temperate mixed forest]]s to [[tropical rainforest]]s. However, in some areas the species can range into [[Secondary forest|second growth]]. In non-breeding times, mountain hawk-eagles may sometimes wander through [[Plain|wooded plains]] and briefly near fairly developed [[village]]s (though usually more secluded and primitive one)  and even cities.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/> In [[Japan]], they reportedly entirely avoid [[peri-urbanisation|woods near urban areas]].<ref name= Naoroji/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Natuhara |first1=Yosihiro |last2=Imai |first2=Chobei |date=1999 |title=Prediction of species richness of breeding birds by landscape-level factors of urban woods in Osaka Prefecture, Japan. |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1023/A:1008869410668 |journal=Biodiversity and Conservation |volume=8 |issue=2 |pages=239–253 |doi=10.1023/A:1008869410668|bibcode=1999BiCon...8..239N |url-access=subscription }}</ref> Elevations the species has been known to live at in the Himalayas are mostly {{convert|600|to|2800|m|ft|abbr=on}} above sea-level. However, they've been recorded at elevations of up to {{convert|4000|m|ft|abbr=on}} in northern [[Yunnan]]. In [[Japan]], they usually reside somewhat lower than in their mainland haunts, typical at between {{convert|250|and|1500|m|ft|abbr=on}}. Mostly only during winter, the species has been recorded (albeit seldom) down to {{convert|200|m|ft|abbr=on}} or even lower as vagrant.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Brazil2/><ref>Fei, W., Luming, L., Jianyun, G., Dao, Y., Wanzhao, H., Ting, Y., Ji, X., Qiang, L. & Xiaojun, Y. (2015). ''Birds of the Ailao Mountains, Yunnan province, China''. Forktail, (31), 47-54.</ref><ref>Murate, T. (2000). ''The home range and habitat use of sub-adults of the Japanese Mountain Hawk-eagle (Spizaetus nipalensis) in Japan''. in Prawiradilaga D. Proceedings Asian Raptor Research Conservation: the Second Symposium on Raptors of Asia, Grand Aquila, Bandung -- Indonesia, 25–27 July 2000: 155-159.</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
[[File:Mountain Hawk-Eagle (36879489254).jpg|thumb|A mountain hawk-eagle stooping down among the trees in [[Uttarakhand]].]]
Mountain hawk-eagles are well adapted to living in forests. As is the case for all ''[[Nisaetus]]'' species, their physical form and flight style is typical of forest-dwelling raptors in general and is often found to be roughly comparable to the features of [[Accipiter|true hawks or ''Accipiters'']] in particular larger species such as the occasionally [[Sympatry|sympatric]] [[Eurasian Goshawk]] (''Accipiter Gentilis''). Like most other forest raptors, mountain hawk-eagles (and ''Nisaetus'' species in general) have a long tail, short broad wings and relatively long but powerful legs, all of which impart greater maneuverability and quicker strike times in denser wooded hunting grounds than other raptorial body plans. The common name hawk-eagle is apparently in reference to their similar adaptations to true hawks.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Brazil2/><ref>Petty, S. J. (1998). ''Ecology and conservation of raptors in forests''. HMSO Publications Centre.</ref> Contrary to the suggestion that, based on their physiology, especially their longer wings and tarsus but shorter talon and bill length, when physically compared to the [[Legge's hawk-eagle]] implies that the mountain hawk-eagle is morphologically adapted to hunting birds more so than mammals, dietary studies indicate that the mountain hawk-eagle is not necessarily a specialized bird predator but rather a generalist and opportunist like many predators.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Gjershaug/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Hertel |first=Fritz |date=October 1995 |title=Ecomorphological Indicators of Feeding Behavior in Recent and Fossil Raptors |url=https://academic.oup.com/auk/article/112/4/890-903/5168306 |journal=The Auk |volume=112 |issue=4 |pages=890–903 |doi=10.2307/4089021|jstor=4089021 |hdl=10211.3/138737 |hdl-access=free |url-access=subscription }}</ref> In fact the small handful of dietary studies of the species show that the mountain hawk-eagle somewhat prefer small [[mammal]]s as prey but readily takes both [[bird]]s and [[reptile]]s given the opportunity.<ref name="Kaneda">{{Cite journal |last=Kaneda |first=Hiroshi |date=December 2009 |title=Prey Selection and Provisioning Rate of a Breeding Pair of Hodgson's Hawk-Eagles Nisaetus nipalensis |journal=Ornithological Science |language=en |volume=8 |issue=2 |pages=151–156 |doi=10.2326/osj.8.151 |issn=1347-0558|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Sun">{{Cite journal |last1=Sun |first1=Yuan-Hsun |last2=Huang |first2=Yung-Kun |last3=Tsai |first3=Wei-Hsun |last4=Hong |first4=Shiao-Yu |last5=Chen |first5=Chao-Chieh |date=June 2009 |title=Breeding-Season Diet of the Mountain Hawk-Eagle in Southern Taiwan |url=http://www.bioone.org/doi/abs/10.3356/JRR-08-86.1 |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |language=en |volume=43 |issue=2 |pages=159–163 |doi=10.3356/JRR-08-86.1 |issn=0892-1016|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Typically, the mountain hawk-eagle tends to still-hunt from a concealed perch in foliage, stooping down to take prey. Most prey is taken on the ground.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> Mountain hawk-eagles have also been observed catching passerines on the wing by giving chase from an ambush or when the prey is flushed by flying low at the [[canopy (biology)|canopy]] level. They will also readily take arboreal mammals and birds from a perch or roost if they're able to fly upon them in an ambush.<ref name= Naoroji/> While most of their prey are relatively small, well within typical prey size range for most raptorial birds, mountain hawk-eagles can take remarkably large prey. Therefore, Brown & Amadon (1986) consider the species as markedly "rapacious and powerful".<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Brown/><ref name= Kaneda/>

One study that reviewed 118 prey items in several nests from southern [[Taiwan]], revealed a surprising preferred prey type for mountain hawk-eagles, [[Petaurista|giant flying squirrel]]s. The [[Indian giant flying squirrel]] (''Petaurista philippensis'') and the [[red and white giant flying squirrel]] (''Petaurista alborufus''), both weighing about {{convert|1.65|kg|lb|abbr=on}} on average, accounted for 47.4% of the food items in Taiwan. How they capture these elusive and nocturnal rodents is not clear, but perhaps the flying squirrels' relatively huge size makes them more conspicuous from the hawk-eagle's lofty perch. A rather smaller true squirrel, the approximately {{convert|277|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Pallas's squirrel]] (''Callosciurus erythraeus''), made up a further 19.5% of the mean diet here. In total, 78.3% of prey taken in the southern Taiwan study was mammalian. However, the fourth most often taken prey species here was the {{convert|1.1|to|1.6|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[Swinhoe's pheasant]] (''Lophura swinhoii''), constituting on average 6.7% of the prey.<ref name= CRC/><ref name= Sun/><ref>Lee, P. F. (1998). ''Body size comparison of two giant flying squirrel species in Taiwan''. Acta Zoologica Taiwanica, 9(1), 51-57.</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Nowak |first1=Ronald M. |title=Walker's mammals of the world |last2=Walker |first2=Ernest P. |date=1999 |publisher=Johns Hopkins Univ. Press |isbn=978-0-8018-5789-8 |location=Baltimore}}</ref> A study in the [[Hyōgo Prefecture]] of [[Japan]] reviewed 142 prey items of a single breeding pair. Quantitatively, most prey deliveries by this pair were rather (almost surprisingly) small in body size, whether this is typical of Japanese hawk-eagles is not clear given the lack of comprehensive study.<ref name= Yamazaki/><ref name= Brazil2/><ref name= Kaneda/> About 17.5% of the nest prey deliveries were unidentified small birds, of an estimated mass of {{convert|17.5|g|oz|abbr=on}} (mostly brought by the male), while 7.7% of prey deliveries were unidentified medium-sized birds, of an estimated mass of {{convert|175|g|oz|abbr=on}}. These were followed by the merely {{convert|20|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Japanese shrew mole]] (''Urotrichus talpoides'') which constituted 7% of prey deliveries (again largely by male) . The most often delivered prey (18.2% of her 44 deliveries) by the female once she resumed hunting were larger class ''[[Elaphe]]'' snakes, in excess of {{convert|100|cm|in|abbr=on}} length and {{convert|325|g|oz|abbr=on}}. Overall, the estimated size of nest prey deliveries here ranged from a {{convert|5|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Japanese grass lizard]] (''Takydromus tachydromoides'') to several [[Japanese hare]]s (''Lepus brachyurus'') weighing an estimated mean of {{convert|1.9|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, the latter presumably constituting a majority of the prey biomass.<ref name= Kaneda/> In another study, the mean size of Japanese hares caught was apparently estimated as somewhat larger at about {{convert|2.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>Takayuki, F., Shin'ichiro, F., Ken, M., Mami, O., Kazuaki, T., Yoshio, S., Tsuneo, S., Yosuke, H. & Manabu, A. (2000). ''Food habits analysis of Hodgson's hawk eagle, Spizaetus nipalensis by CCD camera observation system in the breeding season''. Bull. Fac. Agric. Niigata Univ., 53:71–79.</ref><ref name= Fam/> In [[Jim Corbett National Park]], [[India]], prey reportedly consisted largely of smallish or medium-sized birds (albeit probably larger than those in the above Japanese study) such as [[myna]]s, [[dove]]s, [[Psittacula|parakeet]]s, [[nightjar]]s, [[owl]]s and [[Chicken|village poultry]].<ref name= Naoroji/> In the [[Russian Far East]], apparently the most important prey types were reported as [[Manchurian hare]]s (''Lepus mandshuricus''), a close cousin and of the same size as the Japanese hare, and [[hazel grouse]] (''Tetrastes bonasia''), while most of the remainder of the diet consisted of a mixture of smallish mammals like ([[Mole (animal)|mole]]s, [[Amur hedgehog|hedgehog]]s and [[Siberian flying squirrel|flying squirrel]]s) and largish forest birds ([[White-backed woodpecker|woodpecker]]s, [[Ring-necked pheasant|pheasant]]s and [[Ural owl|owl]]s).<ref name="Nechaev">{{Cite journal |last1=Fam |first1=S. D. |last2=Nijman |first2=V. |date=April 2011 |title=Spizaetus hawk-eagles as predators of arboreal colobines |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/s10329-011-0240-z |journal=Primates |language=en |volume=52 |issue=2 |pages=105–110 |doi=10.1007/s10329-011-0240-z |issn=0032-8332 |pmid= 21340695|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Quantitatively rare prey items that have been recorded have including [[amphibian]]s and, recorded only once, [[fish]].<ref name= Kaneda/><ref>Inglis, C.M. (1902). ''The Birds of the Madhubani sub-division of the Darbhanga District, Tirhut, with notes on species noticed elsewhere in the district, Part IV''. J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc., 14 (3): 554-563.</ref>
[[File:Lepus brachyurus.JPG|thumb|left|Small to medium-sized mammals such as the [[Japanese hare]] seem to form the bulk of the mountain hawk-eagle's diet.]]
While most of the prey mentioned above is of relatively modest size, the mountain hawk-eagle is not infrequently reported to attack prey of quite large sizes, including prey equal to their own size or larger.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Fam>Fam, S. D., & Nijman, V. (2011). ''Spizaetus hawk-eagles as predators of arboreal colobines''. Primates, 52(2), 105-110.</ref> Mountain hawk-eagles have been reported to attack young [[ungulate]]s but often relatively very young and small ones, probably close to a neonatal state. In [[Taiwan]], they took the young of [[Formosan serow]] (''Capricornis swinhoei'') that were estimated to weigh on average under {{convert|2|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Sun/><ref name= Fam/> Of similar size, in [[Japan]], they took young piglets of the [[wild boar]] (''Sus scrofa''), averaging about {{convert|1.75|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Kaneda/> Reportedly, newborn [[sika deer]] (''Cervus nippon''), weighing at least {{convert|4.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, have been preyed upon in Japan as well.<ref name= Berger>Berger, A. J. (1964). ''The Hawking of Japan''.</ref> Scavenging of sika deer killed by human hunters has also been reported.<ref name= Saito>Saito, K. (2009). ''Lead poisoning of Steller's sea-eagle (Haliaeetus pelagicus) and whitetailed eagle (Haliaeetus albicilla) caused by the ingestion of lead bullets and slugs''. Ingestion of lead from spent ammunition: implications for wildlife and humans.</ref> Larger avian prey has been taken by mountain hawk-eagles, including adult [[Indian peafowl]] (''Pavo cristatus'') weighing up to an estimated {{convert|4|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Fam/> In [[Echizen-Kaga Kaigan Quasi-National Park]], [[Japan]], mountain hawk-eagles have been recorded attacking exclusively relatively large [[water bird]]s on several occasions namely: the {{convert|1.12|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[mallard]] (''Anas platyrhynchos''), the {{convert|1.45|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[grey heron]] (''Ardea cinerea''), the {{convert|2.53|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[greater white-fronted goose]] (''Anser albifrons'') and the {{convert|2.77|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[bean goose]] (''Anser fabalis'').<ref name= CRC/><ref>Nakagawa, N., Hironobu, T. & Oohata, K. (2005). ''Mountain Hawk Eagle captures water birds at Katano-Kamoike, a Ramsar site in Ishikawa prefecture, Central Japan''. Strix, 23: 181-183.</ref>
[[File:Nisaetus nipalensis 182186101.jpg|thumb|left|A mountain hawk-eagle in the little-known (and cartographically undescribed) population in [[Primorsky Krai]] in Russia.]]
[[Carnivora]]ns taken by mountain hawk-eagles can also be relatively large as well as potentially dangerous. An estimated {{convert|1.8|kg|lb|abbr=on}} kit of a [[Japanese badger]] (''Meles anakuma'') was preyed upon by a female. Meanwhile, four adult [[Chinese ferret-badger]]s (''Melogale moschata''), weighing on average about {{convert|1.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, were taken in Taiwan, while a [[Japanese marten]] (''Martes melampus'') of the same estimated weight was taken there by a female hawk-eagle.<ref name= Kaneda/><ref name= Sun/><ref name= Fam/> [[Sable]] (''Martes zibellina'') are also likely at threat from these birds.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Miyoshi |first1=Kazuki |last2=Higashi |first2=Seigo |date=2004-12-07 |title=Home range and habitat use by the sable Martes zibellina brachyura in a Japanese cool-temperate mixed forest |url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11284-004-0012-y |journal=Ecological Research |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=95–101 |doi=10.1007/s11284-004-0012-y |issn=0912-3814|url-access=subscription }}</ref> More impressive carnivoran prey dispatched by this species included an adult [[yellow-throated marten]] (''Martes flavigula'') weighing an estimated {{convert|2.75|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, an adult [[red panda]] (''Ailurus fulgens'') weighing an estimated {{convert|4.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and reportedly adult [[raccoon dog]]s (''Nyctereutes procyonoides''), which weigh an average of {{convert|6.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, not to mention (in a similar size range) an occasional [[Cat|domestic cat]] (''Felis silvestris catus'') taken by this species.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Yamazaki/><ref name= Fam/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Wootton |first=J. Timothy |date=July 1987 |title=The effects of body mass, phylogeny, habitat, and trophic level on mammalian age at first reproduction |url=https://academic.oup.com/evolut/article/41/4/732/6870697 |journal=Evolution |language=en |volume=41 |issue=4 |pages=732–749 |doi=10.1111/j.1558-5646.1987.tb05849.x |issn=0014-3820 |jstor=2408884 |pmid= 28564349 |bibcode=1987Evolu..41..732W }}Wootton, J. T. (1987). ''The effects of body mass, phylogeny, habitat, and trophic level on mammalian age at first reproduction''. Evolution, 41(4), 732-749.</ref> Of a similarly impressive nature in size and defensive temperament are [[primate]]s, of which the mountain hawk-eagle is an occasional predator. However, a rather large portion of primate prey, such as [[Old World monkey|monkeys]], are taken as infants or juveniles, and most but not all adults killed by them are perhaps are likely to be previously injured or sickly. Taking even infant monkeys can be provide some risk for hunting hawk-eagles due to the protective nature of mothers as well as the overall monkey troops.<ref name= Fam/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Treves |first=Adrian |date=1999 |title=Has Predation Shaped the Social Systems of Arboreal Primates? |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1023/A:1020532216737 |journal=International Journal of Primatology |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=35–67 |doi=10.1023/A:1020532216737|bibcode=1999IJPri..20...35T |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mcgraw |first1=W. (''Macaca cyclopis'') recorded to be taken in [[Taiwan]] were infants, weighing only an estimated {{convert|500|to|700|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Sun/> An [[Assam macaque]] (''Macaca assamensis'') ===Interspecies predatory relationships===
[[File:Mountain Hawk-eagle - Corbett NP - India 1186 (15340421640).jpg|thumb|right|A mountain hawk-eagle in flight.]]
The mountain hawk-eagle overlaps in distribution with several other eagles, including about three species of ''[[Aquila (genus)|Aquila]]'' and three species of ''[[Sea eagle|Haliaeetus]]'' that are broadly similar in size to them as well as one slightly (in [[Japan]]) to notably (mainland) larger ''Aquila'', the [[golden eagle]] (''Aquila chrysaetos''), and two much larger ''Haliaeetus'' species. However, the mountain hawk-eagle is the largest eagle in its range to live mostly within the confines of forest habitats, thus habitat differences against other larger eagles would normally provide ample partitioning and lessen competition.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/> Some writers have claimed in life history and dietary habits that the mountain hawk-eagle warrants comparison to the top African forest eagle, [[crowned eagle]] (''Stephanoaetus coronatus''). While the mountain hawk-eagle and crowned eagle do show similarities in their territorial display and primary hunting techniques, beyond being larger with proportionately larger feet and talons, the latter is significantly more prone to taking [[primate]]s and to taking extremely large prey (both relative to itself and compared to other eagles).<ref name= Amadon/><ref name= Brown/><ref name= Fam/><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = McGraw |first1=W. S. |last2=Cooke |first2=C. |last3=Shultz |first3=S. |name-list-style=amp | year = 2006 | title = ''Primate remains from African crowned eagle (Stephanoaetus coronatus) nests in Ivory Coast's Tai Forest: implications for primate predation and early hominid taphonomy in South Africa'' | journal = American Journal of Physical Anthropology | volume = 131 | issue = 2| pages = 151–165 | doi=10.1002/ajpa.20420|pmid=16596589 |bibcode=2006AJPA..131..151M }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Shultz |first1=S. |last2=Noë |first2=R. |last3=McGraw |first3=W. S. |last4=Dunbar |first4=R. I. M. | year = 2004 | title = ''A community-level evaluation of the impact of prey behavioural and ecological characteristics on predator diet composition'' | journal = Proceedings of the Royal Society of London B: Biological Sciences | volume = 271 | issue = 1540| pages = 725–732 | doi=10.1098/rspb.2003.2626|pmid=15209106 | pmc = 1691645 |bibcode=2004PBioS.271..725S }}</ref> In some of its range, the mountain hawk-eagle overlaps and shares forests with three other ''[[Nisaetus]]'' species and, often, with various species of ''[[Accipiter]]'', the latter of which lead a comparable lifestyle but are far smaller and more agile. However, usually these other forest raptors can co-exist with the larger raptor by focusing on more generalized and usually smaller prey, largely birds but also [[reptile]]s and [[amphibian]]s, than the mammals seemingly preferred by the mountain hawk-eagle.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Sun/><ref>Li, Y. D. (2011). ''An Introduction to the Raptors of Southeast Asia''. Nature Society (Singapore), Bird Group and Southeast Asian Biodiversity Society, 11-15.</ref> Larger owls sometimes also occur in the mountain hawk-eagles range and are a potential source of competition (excluding the [[fish owl]]s, which are more restricted by diet) despite the temporal partitioning implied in their nocturnal habits. Although the [[Eurasian eagle-owl]] (''Bubo bubo'') usually prefers more open and rockier environments, the little-known [[spot-bellied eagle owl]] (''Bubo nipalensis'') has a very similar central distribution and habitat preferences as the mountain hawk-eagle, and despite being slightly smaller, it goes after exceptionally large prey with perhaps even more aplomb.<ref>{{cite book |last=König |first=Claus |title=''Owls of the World'' |year=2008 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |isbn=978-1-4081-0884-0 |edition=2nd |author2=Weick, Friedhelm }}</ref><ref name= Voous>Voous, K.H. 1988. ''Owls of the Northern Hemisphere''. The MIT Press, 0262220350.</ref>

In Japan, mountain hawk-eagles are regarded as the fourth largest eagle after the [[Steller's sea eagle]] (''Haliaeetus pelagicus''), the [[white-tailed eagle]] (''Haliaeetus albicilla'') and the golden eagle. Despite the [[golden eagle]] race in Japan (''A. c. japonica'') being much smaller than other races and the larger size of Japanese mountain hawk-eagles, the golden eagle here still has a slight size advantage (about 7% larger) and much larger wings which gives them an advantage at fighting and hunting in open air but less maneuverability.<ref name= Brazil2/><ref name= Watson>{{cite book|author=Watson, Jeff |title=''The Golden Eagle''|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aj5MNvCkun0C|year=2010|publisher=A&C Black|isbn=978-1-4081-1420-9}}</ref><ref name= Inoue>Inoue, Y. & Yamazaki, T. (1984). ''Comparative study of food habits between golden eagle Aquila chrysaetos and mountain hawk-eagle Spizaetus nipalensis nestlings''. Aquila chrysaetos 2:14-15.</ref> While the golden is more a bird of open and rocky environments, the two species prey selection overlaps here (probably more so than mainland populations of the two species), with both taking [[Japanese hare]]s supplemented by [[pheasant]]s whenever possible, and this can cause a level of direct competition despite their different preferred habitats.<ref name= Yamazaki/><ref name= Kaneda/><ref name= Inoue/> In at least one case, a golden eagle attacked and may have preyed upon a mountain hawk-eagle.<ref>Ozawa, T., & Imamori, T. 2008. ''Cases of attack by Golden Eagles (Aquila chrysaetos japonica) on Mountain hawk-Eagles (Spizaetus nipalensis) for the purpose of hunting''. Aquila Chrysaetos 22:32-37.</ref> On the other hand, a mountain hawk-eagle may have preyed on the young of the [[black eagle]] (''Ictinaetus malaiensis'') in Taiwan.<ref>Lin, W.-H. & Ho, H.-J. (2010). ''Investigation of Breeding Indian Black Eagle in Fushan Botanical Garden, Northeastern Taiwan, 2004''.</ref> The mountain hawk-eagle is an occasional predator of a wide diversity of owls. Owl prey known to have been taken has included [[Asian barred owlet]] (''Glaucidium cuculoides''), [[jungle owlet]] (''Glaucidium radiatum''), [[brown boobook]] (''Ninox scutulata''), [[Eastern barn owl]] (''Tyto javanica''), [[Ural owl]] (''Strix uralensis'') and, once reportedly, even a [[Eurasian eagle-owl]], a species of similar size and power to the hawk-eagle itself.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Sun/><ref name= Fam/><ref name= Nechaev/><ref name= Osman>Osman S.M. (1975). ''The Mountain Hawk-Eagle''. J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc., 72 (2): 256-270.</ref>

==Breeding==
[[File:０５ 仁別 クマタカの雛巣立つ-28 (2).jpg|thumb|left|A female mountain hawk-eagle in [[Japan]] dismantles prey on her nest.]]
The mountain hawk-eagle maintain their [[home range]] with a rather spectacular aerial display. Display activities tend to peak within the time period prior to breeding. Their aerial display includes conspicuous and often noisy high circling, both single and mutual, and undulating sky dance of steep dives and climbs with bubbling call uttered at each peak. Like many raptors, the display is likely largely to proclaim ownership to conspecifics but also probably has some function in reinforcing existing pair bonds.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Brown/> The breeding season falls between February and June in the [[Himalayas]] while in [[Japan]], it falls from April to July. The laying dates largely correspond to early spring or colder dry season in most of their range.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Brown/>  The pair builds a large stick nest, that can be up to {{convert|1.8|m|ft|abbr=on}} across and {{convert|90|to|120|cm|in|abbr=on}} deep (after repeated uses).<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> Pairs may have as many as 2 to 3 nests but usually have just one. The male in the pair is said to bring most of the nest materials while female is said to primarily construct the nest.<ref name= Brown/> As in many accipitrids, active nests are more often than not lined with greenery, usually either green leaves or conifer sprigs. Nests are usually located at {{convert|12|to|30|m|ft|abbr=on}} above the ground in a large forest tree, though also sometimes more isolated trees such as [[Cedrus deodara|Deodar cedars]] (''Cedrus deodara''), which were popular in the Himalayas region. In the [[Indian subcontinent]], [[Shorea robusta|sal trees]] (''Shorea robusta'') and [[Toona ciliata|red cedar]] (''Toona ciliata'') are favored at slightly lower elevation forests whereas deodar cedars, [[Pinus|pines]], [[holly]], [[Terminalia elliptica|saj]] (''[[Terminalia elliptica]]'') and moru oak (''[[Quercus floribunda]]'') are often favored at higher elevations. Many nests are often near a steep-edged ravine, or alternately near a natural tree line, freshwater wetland or other environment that provides ample view of the surrounding area.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref>Nakanishi, K. (2000). ''The perching behavior of the Mountain Hawk-eagle at defensive observation points''. Proceedings of the First Symposium on Raptors of Asia: 313-319.</ref> Clutch size is usually 1 or 2 but up to 3 eggs in a clutch have been reported in [[Japan]].<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> It is claimed that one egg is considered the norm in most of the range, as is invariably the case in the related [[changeable hawk-eagle]].<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Brown/> The egg is pale clay-colored or reddish in colour with varied freckling of darker red or pure white, and often with blotches and spots of red at the large end.<ref name= Brown/> A sample of egg sizes in the nominate subspecies showed a range of {{convert|65|to|72.7|mm|in|abbr=on}} in height, with an average of {{convert|69.9|mm|in|abbr=on}} while the range in diameter was {{convert|51.2|to|57.4|mm|in|abbr=on}}, with an average of {{convert|53.8|mm|in|abbr=on}}. One egg from ''N. n. orientalis'' measured {{convert|69|x|56|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Brown/> It has been claimed that only the female will incubate and will be fed by the male.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Brown/> Hatching dates seem to peak around mid-March in the [[Indian subcontinent]].<ref name= Naoroji/> At one nest, an immature male was recorded as the mate of an adult female.<ref name= Osman/> In another case, when the female in a pair died during nesting, the following year the male paired with another female and used a nest {{convert|200|m|ft|abbr=on}} from the original nest.<ref name= Naoroji/>
[[File:Nisaetus nipalensis 208017131.jpg|thumb|right|An adult mountain hawk-eagle.]]
As is typical of accipitrids, the female takes by far the primary role in brooding and protecting the young, while the male makes prey deliveries into the nest or the nearby nest vicinity. Reportedly, the female is very aggressive if nest is disturbed but male is less so or not at all. The aggressiveness of the female may rival that of the often co-occurring [[spot-bellied eagle-owl]] and even outrival the defensive attacks on human by the more powerful African [[crowned eagle]].<ref name= Brown/><ref>Hume, R. (1991). ''Owls of the world''. Running Press, Philadelphia, PA. 1991.</ref> Cousins such as the [[Legge's hawk-eagle|Legge's]] and [[changeable hawk-eagle]] do not typically display any aggression or, if so, are very mild in protective behaviour towards humans while nesting.<ref name= Naoroji/> Apparently, wood-cutters in particular often attract the ire of the female mountain hawk-eagle.<ref name= Naoroji/> Unlike attacks on humans by crowned eagles and [[northern goshawk]]s, the attack of mountain hawk-eagle is unlikely to be deterred either by traveling in parties or counterattack. Even when struck with branches, machetes or fist and hit with buckshot by humans, apparently the female will still not cease her attack unless killed or grievously injured.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref>Brown, L. (1977). ''Eagles of the World''. Universe Books.</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Parker |first1= J. W. |year= 1999 |title= ''Raptor attacks on people'' |journal= Journal of Raptor Research |volume= 33 |pages= 63–66 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Montgomerie |first1= R. D. |last2= Weatherhead |first2= P. J. |year= 1988 |title= ''Risks and rewards of nest defence by parent birds'' |journal= The Quarterly Review of Biology |volume= 63 |issue= 2|pages= 167–187 |doi=10.1086/415838|s2cid= 53314360 }}</ref> In one case, a local woman in the [[Kumaon division]] of northern India fell victim to a "particularly savage" attack by a female mountain hawk-eagle and subsequently died from the injuries sustained.<ref name= Naoroji/> Cases have been observed in north India where [[rhesus macaque]]s, [[northern plains gray langur]]s (''Semnopithecus entellus'') and [[yellow-throated marten]]s, all known nest predators, have been driven off by the female hawk-eagle, in the case of the marten while repeatedly raking the back as it ran off.<ref name= Naoroji/> The males may make up to two prey deliveries each day but in the area of nests around disturbed village-side forest in [[India]] seemed to have problems procuring a sufficient amount of prey. Caches of food may be kept during incubation and the early nestling stage but generally cease thereafter.<ref name= Naoroji/> After about three weeks, the young are more active and may engage in wing stretching and flapping. At this point, the female takes to a perch about {{convert|50|to|100|m|ft|abbr=on}} away but continues to watchfully protect the young. The young may soon also be able to feed themselves but are often apparently fed by the mother well after this.<ref name= Naoroji/> Reportedly family remains together for some time after young fly and the young eagle is fed until they can fly more strongly.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> In Japan, an eaglet that hatched in April flew by the end of June.<ref>Tubokawa, M. (2004). ''[The breeding biology of the Hodgson's Hawk Eagle in eastern Hokkaido, Japan]''. Strix 22, 9-69.</ref> In India, the minimum amount of time from hatching to leaving the nest was claimed as 53 days.<ref name= Naoroji/> The breeding cycle lasts for at least 80 days.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/>

==In human culture==
The eagle's feathers are coveted among indigenous [[Paiwan people|Paiwan]] and [[Rukai people|Rukai]] peoples of Taiwan; the Rukai especially regard it as sacred being a reincarnation of the [[Deinagkistrodon acutus|sharp-nosed viper]] who guided their ancestors.<ref>{{cite thesis|pages=313–4|year=2015|author=Hueiyun Chen|degree=PhD|publisher=The Australian National University |title=Form and Meaning in Paiwanese Art and Material Culture|hdl=1885/106006 |url=http://hdl.handle.net/1885/106006}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author1=Sasala Taiban |author2=Jun-lin Chen |author3=Yun-hsun Sun |title=Whose Feathers? Changes and Conflicts in the Use of Mountain Hawk-eagle Feathers among Rukai Communities in Taiwan |journal=Journal of Archaeology and Anthropology |date=December 2019 |issue=91 |pages=85–122 |doi=10.6152/jaa.201912_(91).0003 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]]}}</ref>

==Status==
[[File:Spizaetus nipalensis.jpg|thumb|right|A captive specimen of the threatened Japanese subspecies, ''N. n. orientalis''.]]
Though it is not considered a globally [[threatened species]], the mountain hawk-eagle is never more than uncommon to rare locally. It occurs over a rather large distributional range that apparently extends over 19 million square kilometers. However, in estimations from the late 1990s, it was considered doubtful that the density of mountain hawk-eagle was high enough to reach 10,000 individuals, even with the now-separated [[Legge's hawk-eagle]] included at that time and all juveniles.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/> High levels of various habitat degradation and [[deforestation]] is probably the primary driver of declines throughout the range. In particular, the increasing density of human populations in northern [[India]], southeastern [[China]] and [[Japan]] are likely to continue to facilitate declines in forest quantity and quality.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref>Shing, L. K., Lau, M. W., Fellowes, J. R., & Lok, C. B. P. (2006). ''Forest bird fauna of South China: notes on current distribution and status''. Forktail, 22, 23.</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Nagaike |first=T. |title=Restoration of Boreal and Temperate Forests |chapter-url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/9781482211979/chapters/10.1201/b18809-22 |chapter=Restoration of Conifer Plantations in Japan: Perspectives for Stand and Landscape Management and for Enabling Social Participation |date=2015-08-14 |publisher=CRC Press |isbn=978-0-429-16010-3 |editor-last=Stanturf |editor-first=John A. |edition=0 |pages=380–391 |language=en |doi=10.1201/b18809-22}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Iida |first1=Tomohiko |last2=Mori |first2=Takayuki |last3=Inoue |first3=Susumu |date=2006-10-27 |title=The Relation between the Fall in Breeding Success Ratio of Mountain Hawk Eagle (Spizaetus nipalensis) and Forest Structure of its Home Range |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2324/9245 |journal=Journal of the Faculty of Agriculture, Kyushu University |language=en |volume=51 |issue=2 |pages=293–301 |doi=10.5109/9245 |hdl=2324/9245 |issn=0023-6152|hdl-access=free }}</ref> Likely more localized and minor threats from humans are persecution as occasional killers of domestic poultry. Despite their popularity in Asian [[falconry]], it is unlikely gathering of hawk-eagles for this is a significant problem.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref>Meyburg, B. U. (1986). ''Threatened and near-threatened diurnal birds of prey of the world''. Birds of Prey Bull, 3(1), 12.</ref> [[Lead poisoning]] from consuming carcasses of [[sika deer]], left there by human hunters using lead bullets, have resulted in the death of some mountain hawk-eagles.<ref name= Saito/> Mountain hawk-eagles may be vulnerable to viruses and helminths as well.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Onuma |first1=Manabu |last2=Kakogawa |first2=Masayoshi |last3=Yanagisawa |first3=Masae |last4=Haga |first4=Atsushi |last5=Okano |first5=Tomomi |last6=Neagari |first6=Yasuko |last7=Okano |first7=Tsukasa |last8=Goka |first8=Koichi |last9=Asakawa |first9=Mitsuhiko |date=2017 |title=Characterizing the temporal patterns of avian influenza virus introduction into Japan by migratory birds |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/jvms/79/5/79_16-0604/_article |journal=Journal of Veterinary Medical Science |language=en |volume=79 |issue=5 |pages=943–951 |doi=10.1292/jvms.16-0604 |issn=0916-7250 |pmc=5447987 |pmid=28484128}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Moriguchi |first1=Sachiko |last2=Onuma |first2=Manabu |last3=Goka |first3=Koichi |date=2016 |title=Spatial assessment of the potential risk of avian influenza A virus infection in three raptor species in Japan |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/jvms/78/7/78_15-0551/_article |journal=Journal of Veterinary Medical Science |language=en |volume=78 |issue=7 |pages=1107–1115 |doi=10.1292/jvms.15-0551 |issn=0916-7250 |pmc=4976265 |pmid=26972333}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Okano |first1=Tsukasa |last2=Matsuura |first2=Satoko |last3=Kubo |first3=Masahito |last4=Yanai |first4=Tokuma |last5=Asano |first5=Makoto |last6=Suzuki |first6=Masatsugu |date=2010 |title=Renal Adenocarcinoma in a Mountain Hawk Eagle (Spizaetus nipalensis) Rescued for Traffic Accident |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/jjzwm/15/2/15_2_105/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=Japanese Journal of Zoo and Wildlife Medicine |language=en |volume=15 |issue=2 |pages=105–109 |doi=10.5686/jjzwm.15.105 |issn=1342-6133|doi-access=free }}</ref> Similar threats are faced by all ''[[Nisaetus]]'' hawk-eagles, with only the [[changeable hawk-eagle]] shown to be resilient to human interferences and not in decline at the species level.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /><ref name="Gjershaug, J. O. 2006" /> The Japanese population of mountain hawk-eagles is particularly showing signs of decline. In the 1990s, the Japanese population was estimated at 900–1,000 total individuals and may have reduced even more so today from that figure.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /><ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Asai/><ref>Collar, N. J., Andreev, A. V., Chan, S., Crosby, M. J., Subramanya, S., & Tobias, J. A. (2001). ''Threatened birds of Asia: the BirdLife International red data book'' (No. 598.2095 T531). Birdlife International, Cambridge (RU).</ref><ref>Goto, M., & Maegata, M. (2010). ''Necessity of a policy standard for Red List of Threatened Species birds: the case of Spizaetus nipalensis''. Memoirs of the Faculty of Agriculture of Kinki University, 43, 37-45.</ref> As the species is a [[r/K selection theory|K-strategist]] like all eagles, it was feared that the ongoing population reduction of ''N. n. orientalis'' might lead to loss of genetic diversity, and consequently [[inbreeding depression]]. However, genetic diversity was shown to be still considerable at present.<ref name= Asai/>

熊鷹（學名：Nisaetus nipalensis；排灣語：qadris），又名熊鹰、山鷹鵰、赫氏角鹰、赫氏鷹鵰、亞洲鷹鵰、山地鷹鵰，是鷹科亞洲鷹鵰屬的猛禽，一般棲息於中高海拔的常绿森林，种加词取自指名亚种的主产地尼泊尔。


外觀
熊鷹是一種偏大的鷹科猛禽，一般身長63至80公分，翼展140至165公分。成年的熊鷹的上半身呈棕色，有白色的下半身、羽毛和尾巴。牠們的胸部、腹部和後翅有很明顯的黑白（或棕白）相間條紋。牠們的翅膀很寬，在飛行時呈V型，熊鷹的亞成鳥通常有白色頭部。


生物學
熊鷹通常分布1,000～3,000公尺左右之中高海拔原始闊葉林或針闊混合林內，牠們一般會在樹上築巢，每次產卵只產下一顆卵。通常以小型哺乳動物、鳥類和爬行動物為食。。其族群數量極稀少，獵食時大多採伏擊方式，於視野遼闊之枝椏上俯瞰，當獵物出現時，即悄然滑行後接近獵物再急速俯衝突擊。


亚种
鷹鵰指名亚种（学名：Nisaetus nipalensis nipalensis Hodgson, 1836）。分布於巴基斯坦東北部、喜馬拉雅山脈、中國雲貴高原至東南丘陵與海南島一帶、台灣、中南半島北部山區至緬甸南部的德林達依省及馬來西亞的北部與蘭卡威等地區。
鷹鵰东方亚种（学名：Nisaetus nipalensis orientalis （Temminck & Schlegel, 1844） ）。主要分布於日本，可能也分布於中國東北與內蒙古、朝鮮半島與俄羅斯遠東地區。该物种的模式产地在日本。
鷹鵰印南亚种（学名：Nisaetus nipalensis kelaarti （Legge, 1878） ）。分布於印度西高止山脈與斯里蘭卡。


與人類關係
族群稀少，且為養鷹者熱衷的對象，賴以生存之中、高海拔原始森林頻遭砍伐及人為開發壓力，本種的生存岌岌可危，在台灣為第一級保育類。
熊鷹在台灣原住民排灣族及魯凱族文化中有重要意涵。如部分排灣族地區的神話傳說中，族人往生後會化身為百步蛇，百步蛇年邁凋零後身上會漸漸長出羽毛，死後化為熊鷹；排灣族群相傳熊鷹為了不讓羽毛被人類直接拔去，預知生命臨終前會以急速飛行向山壁衝撞，讓羽毛自然脫落，而非直接拔下。排灣族人頭飾上的羽飾即為貴族身份以上階級象徵，若拾獲羽毛必須將其給部落王族或貴族過目，因此規範禁獵熊鷹。
